scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the administrative procedures of the Athenian archon regarding the funding of dramatic festivals.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Aristotle's likely composition of the Poetics (c. -335), documenting the established state-funded system of his time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule concerns the professional division of labor in the Athenian theater during the 4th century BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Aristotle at his floruit (-335) as a contemporary observation of the theatrical profession.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle refers to the regulation of Athenian dramatic or oratorical contests using water clocks.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Though mentioning 'other days', Aristotle is discussing the history and regulation of competitions in his own society around -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Refers to the competitive dynamics of the Athenian theater and the social pressure from contemporary actors.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes a common practice among 'good poets' and 'bad poets' at the time of his writing in -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The distinction between poetry and history reflects the intellectual categories and professional roles in Aristotle's Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle treats this as a contemporary philosophical and aesthetic principle in -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The convention of naming in tragedy vs comedy is a specific characteristic of Greek drama in Aristotle's era.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This describes the prevailing conventions of dramatic composition during Aristotle's life in -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule on appropriate female characterization reflects Athenian gender norms and their application to the stage.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle documents the social and aesthetic expectations of his contemporary society in -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle expresses the hierarchical social evaluation of women and slaves typical of 4th-c. Athenian thought.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The text reflects the contemporary socio-moral hierarchy as Aristotle understood it in -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle evaluates slaves' moral status based on the social and legal structure of 4th-c. Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the prevailing societal norms and status of slaves in Aristotle's time around -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The method of poetic composition through simulation is presented as a professional technique within the Greek tradition.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle presents this as a normative psychological observation for poets in -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule concerns the evolution and role of the chorus in Athenian tragedies.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle critiques contemporary practices (like Agathon's interludes) while writing in -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the intellectual and social boundaries between poetry and grammar in Aristotle's Greece.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle defends the contemporary autonomy of poetic art from sophistic criticism in -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The right of poets to alter words is a recognized linguistic convention in Aristotle's literary culture.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle argues against contemporary critics of his era (like Eucleides) in -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The generic conventions of Epic vs Tragedy were foundational to the literary landscape of 4th-c. Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This is a contemporary generic analysis written by Aristotle in -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The relationship between meter and diction in epic is a norm of Greek poetic theory.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes the functional norms of hexameter as understood in his time (-335).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The strict adherence to hexameter for epic is described as a settled convention in Greek literature.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle identifies this as a rule proven by 'experience' and 'Nature' up to his writing in -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The distinction between poetic and technical error is part of Aristotle's contemporary aesthetic theory.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Established by Aristotle as a rule of his own poetic science in -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The standard of 'ideal' representation reflects the artistic goals of 4th-c. Greek painters and poets.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle uses contemporary (Zeuxis) and historical examples to define a current rule in -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Society's allowance for poetic diction and metaphors is a professional license described by Aristotle.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This identifies the latitude given to poets by the contemporary audience and critics in -335.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Criticism of actors for failing to portray high-status women appropriately reflects Athenian social decorum.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle mentions criticism of 'modern actors,' referring to his own 4th-c. context in -335.
